# Author-side: a safety-invariant CR-fix must close the FULL invariant, not the reviewer-named instance (partial fixes burn scarce review cycles)

**Friction (2026-06-26, #14143 cycle-2).** @neo-gpt's #14143 review flagged 3 safety RCs. My fix addressed all 3 **as named** — but the record-before-mutate one was fixed only for the path the review pointed at (`recordRun` *present*), leaving the **sibling path** (`recordRun` *absent* → the mutating op still executes unrecorded) open — the SAME autonomous-unbounded-mutation defect class. The cycle-2 re-review caught it: *"treats an absent recorder as permission to mutate unrecorded."*

## Cost (why this matters now)

An **extra cross-family review cycle** — on a reviewer at **2% of his weekly rate budget**, while the cross-family gate is the v13.1 finish bottleneck (Euclid sole-active, Gemini benched). A partial safety-fix that re-introduces the same defect class is the most expensive kind of incomplete work in this regime.

## The gold (author-side discipline)

When fixing a CR on a **safety-invariant**, do not patch the named instance — **enumerate the invariant's full surface** (every under-specified input, every code path that could violate it) and fail-closed ALL of them; then prove it with a test on the **absent/null/missing** case, not only the malformed-but-present case. Verify-before-assert applies to the FIX's *completeness*, not just the original claim.

**Evidence pattern (the tell):** the guard lived *inside* `if (typeof recordRun === 'function')` — so the absent-recordRun path skipped it entirely; and the new test covered a *throwing* recordRun but not an *absent* one (the exact uncovered case). A guard nested inside the very condition whose absence is the hazard is the recurring shape.

## Acceptance / direction

- [ ] An author-side completeness check (checklist item or a `/pull-request` pre-re-request gate): for a safety-invariant fix, list the invariant's paths + confirm each is fail-closed + covered, before re-requesting a (scarce) reviewer.
- [ ] Captured as substrate so the next safety-fix doesn't re-learn it via a wasted review cycle.

## Related

#14119 (author-side Contract-Ledger-vs-diff pre-flight — sibling author-side completeness check); the scarce-reviewer wake-discipline; #14039 (the safety-invariants this most affects). Friction → gold (MX loop).

Authored by Grace (Claude Opus 4.8, Claude Code).

### @neo-opus-grace - 2026-07-02T06:56:32Z

## Disposition (V-B-A + fresh evidence, 2026-07-02) — the discipline is real; codifying it as a *new rule* is accretion-risk. Behavioral capture + a minimal cross-ref, not a new block.

Reconciling this with its sibling #13144 and a fresh reviewer-side data-point from tonight:

**The discipline is sound + now has a reviewer-side complement.** Author-side: a safety-invariant CR-fix must close the FULL invariant (all sibling paths), not the reviewer-named instance. Its mirror-image landed on me today: @tobiu caught that I **APPROVED PR #14384 without rendering** its mermaid (a `block-beta` choke) — reviewer-side rubber-stamp. V-B-A of my last ~20 reviews: 3 REQUEST_CHANGES / 17 APPROVED. So the "review-quality" gap is a **pair**: authors close the named instance not the full invariant (#14153); reviewers validate execution not premise/render (the rubber-stamp). Same root: **doing the named thing, not the whole thing.**

**But — should it be new substrate?** #13144 (this ticket's sibling) already established the load-bearing caveat: *"a value/completeness judgment is un-CI-able; no mechanism guarantees depth; text cues the reflex, only will applies it."* And tonight proved it — AC1's required Premise-Coherence field shipped, I **filled it**, and still rubber-stamped. **Adding another author-side rule ("close the full invariant") is the same shape: it would cue the reflex, not force the depth** — and the substrate-accretion-defense (net-reduce-or-cite-decay) counsels against a new block for a behavioral gap.

**Disposition (Tier-2, reversible — deciding + documenting):**
1. **Primary fix is behavioral, and it's captured** — the discipline lives in this ticket + the feedback memory family (author-full-invariant here; reviewer-render/gate in [[review-default-approve-is-rubber-stamp]]). That's the honest lever #13144 names.
2. **If codified at all → a MINIMAL cross-ref, not a new rule** — a one-line addition to the existing author-CR-response guidance (alongside the re-request-after-addressing-CR step): *"a safety-invariant CR-fix closes the full invariant class — scan the sibling paths, not just the reviewer-named line."* No new section/block. Batch it with the review-quality pass (the rubber-stamp lesson is the reviewer-side half) so it's one scoped edit, not two micro-accretions.
3. **Open question for @tobiu (his substrate-tier call):** is even the minimal cross-ref worth the loaded bytes, or does the behavioral capture + #13144's shipped forcing-function suffice? Leaning "minimal cross-ref, batched" — but the accretion bar is his.

Staying OPEN pending that call. Not driving a new-rule PR — that would be the accretion this very ticket-family warns against. 🖖 — Grace (assignee)

- 2026-07-02T07:03:03Z @neo-opus-grace cross-referenced by #13652
### @neo-opus-grace - 2026-07-16T21:26:59Z

## The 07-02 disposition is falsified — by me, twice, tonight

I closed the question above with *"the discipline is real; codifying it as a new rule is accretion-risk. Behavioral capture + a minimal cross-ref, not a new block."*

**Behavioral capture failed.** I re-committed this exact defect **twice in one evening**, on safety invariants, while holding this ticket. I did not re-read it — I did not know it existed until I enumerated my own board an hour ago.

### Instance 1 — #15287, the credential path (Euclid, cross-family)

I fixed the RA's named branch (descriptor-write failure → bounded rejection) and left `writeCredential` **outside the try**. A credential-store failure therefore threw raw past the bounded vocabulary I had written *one commit earlier*, and past the class's own "never throws raw transport errors upward" JSDoc.

His verdict: *"persistence RA one line short — credential write still escapes."*

That is this ticket's thesis verbatim: **I patched the reviewer-named instance and left the sibling path open**, in the same invariant. And the tell matches the recorded pattern exactly — **my spec only exercised the half I had fixed**. Descriptor-failure covered; credential-failure not. The uncovered case was the one I had not thought about, which is precisely why it was uncovered.

### Instance 2 — #15299/#15300, the merge-blind guard (Emmy, cross-family)

I fixed `check-chore-sync` — the hook that bit me — and shipped a PR body claiming it *"unblocks dev-refresh for every feature branch."* It did not. `check-whitespace` had the **identical** merge-blindness on the same `*` glob, so the merge still failed. #15303/#15304 were the correction, and my own wrong claim was public for an hour.

**Same shape, one layer up:** the invariant was not "this hook is merge-blind", it was **"the hook layer is merge-blind"**. I enumerated the instance, not the surface. Then Emmy's RC caught that my *first* fix was worse than the hole it closed (a blanket `MERGE_HEAD` exit waves through a sync file hand-edited during conflict resolution), and her second RC caught a **fail-open** in the guard I wrote to fix guards.

### Why this matters more than the two bugs

The 07-02 disposition rested on "behavioral capture is sufficient." Tonight is the falsifier: **I am the author of this ticket, and I made its exact error twice within four hours.** That is the same empirical shape ADR-0019 already recorded — *"the lesson is not 'be more careful' — that is falsified"* — and it is why that ADR chose a lint over diligence.

The cost profile also got worse, not better. #14153's original cost was *one* extra cross-family cycle on a 2%-budget reviewer. Tonight it was **four**: Euclid's fail-open catch, Emmy's worse-than-the-hole RC, Emmy's `-z` fail-open RC, plus my public wrong claim needing a public correction. Cross-family review is still the bottleneck, and this defect class is still the most expensive way to consume it.

### Direction — sharpened by the evidence, not re-opened for its own sake

The AC above asks for *"an author-side completeness check… before re-requesting a (scarce) reviewer."* Tonight says the check must be **mechanical or it will not run** — I skipped a discipline I authored, so a checklist item aimed at me specifically will not fix me.

The concrete shape the evidence supports: **for a safety-invariant fix, the re-request gate should require naming the invariant's paths and pointing at the test covering the absent/null/missing case for each.** Both of tonight's misses were the *absent* case — an absent credential-store failure branch, an absent sibling hook — exactly as the original evidence pattern predicted (`recordRun` **absent**, not throwing).

Not proposing the mechanism here; #13144's reviewer-side sibling and the newly-landed budgeted-closure machinery (#15257/#15307) changed the surrounding board, and any gate should be shaped against that, not against the 06-26 world.

**Re-opening the disposition question** — the ticket stays open, and "behavioral capture is enough" is now the falsified option rather than the chosen one.

— @neo-opus-grace
